# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark construction details and experimental results about repository-level context files for coding agents, including task counts, evaluation settings, success-rate changes, cost changes, behavioral trace findings, and stated limitations.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: method
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 1 / Section 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
AGENTbench contains 138 Python software-engineering instances drawn from 12 repositories with developer-written context files.

## Source Excerpt

"The benchmark contains 138 138 unique instances, covering both bug-fixing and feature addition tasks across 12 12 recent and niche repositories, which all feature developer-written context files."

## Note

This is a dataset construction fact, not a performance claim.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 3.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The authors kept only PRs whose environment setup produced at least one passing test, which covered 87% of filtered instances.

## Source Excerpt

"We only keep PRs where the resulting dictionary contains at least one passing test, which corresponds to 87 % 87\% of the filtered instances."

## Note

This evidence describes a benchmark filtering rule.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 3.2 / Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The final AGENTbench tests achieved 75% average coverage of modified code.

## Source Excerpt

"The resulting tests achieve an average coverage of 75% of the modified code (see Table ˜ 1 )."

## Note

The same table reports a minimum of 2.5% and a maximum of 100% coverage.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
LLM-generated context files reduced average resolution rate by 0.5% on SWE-bench Lite and 2% on AGENTbench.

## Source Excerpt

"LLM-generated context files cause performance drops in 5 out of 8 settings across SWE-bench Lite and AGENTbench... In more detail, the average resolution rate is reduced by 0.5 % 0.5\% and 2 % 2\% on average on SWE-bench Lite and AGENTbench , respectively."

## Note

This finding conflicts with the paper’s described industry recommendation pattern that encourages context files.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.2 / Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
LLM-generated context files increased average steps by 2.45 on SWE-bench Lite and 3.92 on AGENTbench, with average cost increases of 20% and 23%.

## Source Excerpt

"Meanwhile, the context files increase the # steps in every setting on average by 2.45 2.45 and 3.92 3.92 steps, respectively, which leads to a cost increase of 20% and 23% on average, respectively (see Table ˜ 2 )."

## Note

This evidence shows higher cost alongside lower average success for LLM-generated context files.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Abstract / Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Developer-provided context files improved performance only marginally relative to omitting context files.

## Source Excerpt

"Surprisingly, we observe that developer-provided files only marginally improve performance compared to omitting them entirely (an increase of 4% on average)..."
"We find that all context files consistently increase the number of steps required to complete tasks... developer-written ones provide a marginal performance gain."

## Note

The source describes the gain as marginal rather than large or consistent across all agents.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Developer-provided context files increased average steps by 3.34 and increased cost by as much as 19%.

## Source Excerpt

"However, developer-provided context files also increase the average number of steps and costs required to solve the task, on average by 3.34 3.34 steps and at most 19%, respectively."

## Note

This evidence is about cost and step overhead, not about success-rate direction.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Context files did not meaningfully reduce the number of steps before agents first touched files that were modified in the original PR patch.

## Source Excerpt

"To assess the usefulness of these overviews, we measure how quickly agents discover files relevant to the described issue... Both on SWE-bench Lite and AGENTbench the presence of context files does not meaningfully reduce this metric, as shown in Figure ˜ 4 ."

## Note

This evidence bounds the claimed repository-overview value of context files rather than explaining why they fail to help.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: incident
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
In manual trace inspection, GPT-5.1 mini sometimes searched for and reread context files even though those files were already present in its context.

## Source Excerpt

"While context files appear to increase the number of required steps significantly for GPT-5.1 mini , we observe in manual trace inspection that this increase is due to it (i) issuing multiple commands to find the context files and (ii) reading them (multiple times) despite them being already included in the agent’s context."

## Note

This is a model-behavior observation from manual trace inspection, not a universal mechanism claim for all agents.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
When other documentation was removed, LLM-generated context files improved performance by 2.7% on average and outperformed developer-written documentation.

## Source Excerpt

"In this setting, where context files are the only source of documentation available, LLM-generated context files not only consistently improve performance by 2.7 2.7 % on average, but also outperform developer-written documentation."

## Note

This result is conditional on documentation removal and does not describe the main evaluation setting.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
With context files present, agents ran more tests, searched more files, read more files, wrote more files, and used more repository-specific tooling.

## Source Excerpt

"We find that, across all models, when context files are present, the coding agents run more tests. They also tend to navigate the repository more: they search more files ( grep ), read more files, and write more files. Lastly, adding context files causes agents to use more repository-specific tooling..."

## Note

This evidence directly describes an observed behavioral pathway associated with context-file presence.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.3 / Section 5
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
When tool names were mentioned in context files, agents used those tools much more often, and the paper notes Python-heavy evaluation as a limitation because repository knowledge may already be in model parameters.

## Source Excerpt

"For instance, uv is used 1.6 times per instance on average when mentioned in the context files, compared to fewer than 0.01 times when it is not mentioned, and repository-specific tools are used 2.5 times per instance on average when mentioned, compared to fewer than 0.05 times when they are not mentioned."
"The current evaluation is focused heavily on Python. Since this is a language that is widely represented in the training data, much detailed knowledge about tooling, dependencies, and other repository specifics might be present in the models’ parametric knowledge, nullifying the effect of context files."

## Note

The first passage is an observed instruction-following effect; the second is a stated scope limitation that bounds how broadly the results may generalize.
---

"While context files appear to increase the number of required steps significantly for GPT-5.1 mini , we observe in manual trace inspection that this increase is due to it (i) issuing multiple commands to find the context files and (ii) reading them (multiple times) despite them being already included in the agent’s context."

## Note

This is a model-behavior observation from manual trace inspection, not a universal mechanism claim for all agents.
```
